So send to Joppa and summon Simon, also called Peter. He's crashing at the house of a tanner named Simon, by the seaside. When he shows up, he'll talk to you.'

web_text: Therefore I sent to you at once, and it was good of you to come. Now therefore we are all here present in the sight of God to hear all things that have been commanded you by God.” Peter opened his mouth and said, “Truly I perceive that God doesn’t show favoritism; but in every nation he who fears him and works righteousness is acceptable to him. The word which he sent to the children of Israel, preaching good news of peace by Jesus Christ—he is Lord of all—
slang_text: So I sent for you pronto, and it was totally righteous of you to come. Now here we all are, standing in front of God, ready to hear whatever it is God's commanded you to tell us." Peter opens his mouth and goes, "For real, now I get it -- God doesn't play favorites. No duh, in every single nation, whoever fears him and does what's right is totally cool with him. That's the message he sent to the kids of Israel, spreadin' the good news of peace through Jesus Christ -- and get this, he's Lord over everybody, no exceptions --

web_text: you yourselves know what happened, which was proclaimed throughout all Judea, beginning from Galilee, after the baptism which John preached; even Jesus of Nazareth, how God anointed him with the Holy Spirit and with power, who went about doing good and healing all who were oppressed by the devil, for God was with him. We are witnesses of everything he did both in the country of the Jews, and in Jerusalem; whom they also killed, hanging him on a tree. God raised him up the third day, and gave him to be revealed,
slang_text: you guys already know what went down, the stuff that got announced all through Judea, startin' up in Galilee after the baptism John was preachin' -- Jesus of Nazareth, how God anointed him with the Holy Spirit and hella power, and he went around doin' good and healin' everybody bein' pushed around by the devil, 'cause God was totally with him. We saw it all firsthand, both out in Jewish country and in Jerusalem -- and yeah, they killed him, hung him up on a tree. But God raised him back up on the third day, epic as it gets, and let him be seen,

web_text: not to all the people, but to witnesses who were chosen before by God, to us, who ate and drank with him after he rose from the dead. He commanded us to preach to the people and to testify that this is he who is appointed by God as the Judge of the living and the dead. All the prophets testify about him, that through his name everyone who believes in him will receive remission of sins.” While Peter was still speaking these words, the Holy Spirit fell on all those who heard the word.
slang_text: not to everybody, just to witnesses God picked out ahead of time -- us, the ones who ate and drank with him after he came back from the dead. He told us to preach to the people and testify that this is the guy God appointed as Judge over the living and the dead. All the prophets back him up too, sayin' that through his name, anybody who believes in him gets total forgiveness of sins." And while Peter's still layin' this down, boom -- the Holy Spirit falls on every last person listening to the word. Cowabunga, no other word for it.

web_text:
They of the circumcision who believed were amazed, as many as came with Peter, because the gift of the Holy Spirit was also poured out on the Gentiles. For they heard them speaking in other languages and magnifying God.
Then Peter answered, “Can anyone forbid these people from being baptized with water? They have received the Holy Spirit just like us.” He commanded them to be baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they asked him to stay some days.
slang_text: The Jewish believers who came with Peter were totally floored, 'cause the gift of the Holy Spirit got poured out on the Gentiles too -- as if that wasn't wild enough. They heard 'em speaking in other languages and giving God major praise. So Peter goes, "Can anybody talk to the hand on this? Who's gonna stop these folks from gettin' baptized with water? They got the Holy Spirit just like us, fer sure." So he had 'em baptized in the name of Jesus Christ. Then they begged him to hang around a few more days -- and honestly, why wouldn't he.

web_text: Now the apostles and the brothers who were in Judea heard that the Gentiles had also received the word of God. When Peter had come up to Jerusalem, those who were of the circumcision contended with him, saying, “You went in to uncircumcised men, and ate with them!” But Peter began, and explained to them in order, saying,
slang_text: So word got out to the apostles and the brothers back in Judea that the Gentiles had scored the word of God too, fer sure. When Peter bounced up to Jerusalem, the circumcision crew jumped all over him, going, "Say what? You waltzed right into some uncircumcised dudes' house and chowed down with 'em?!" Way harsh. But Peter didn't spaz -- he just laid it all out for 'em, step by step.

web_text:
“I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision: a certain container descending, like it was a great sheet let down from heaven by four corners. It came as far as me. When I had looked intently at it, I considered, and saw the four-footed animals of the earth, wild animals, creeping things, and birds of the sky. I also heard a voice saying to me,
‘Rise, Peter, kill and eat!’ But I said, ‘Not so, Lord, for nothing unholy or unclean has ever entered into my mouth.’
slang_text:
"So there I was in Joppa, just praying, when I zone out into this trance and get a totally gnarly vision: some kind of container coming down, like a great big sheet lowered from heaven by its four corners. It floats right down to me. I lean in and check it out close, and there's all these four-footed critters, wild animals, creepy-crawlies, and birds -- the whole zoo. Then I hear a voice going,
'Get up, Peter, kill it and eat it!' And I'm like, 'No way, Lord, nothing grody or unclean has ever touched my mouth, not once.'

web_text:
But a voice answered me the second time out of heaven,
‘What God has cleansed, don’t you call unclean.’ This was done three times, and all were drawn up again into heaven. Behold, immediately three men stood before the house where I was, having been sent from Caesarea to me. The Spirit told me to go with them, without discriminating. These six brothers also accompanied me, and we entered into the man’s house.
slang_text:
But the voice comes back at me a second time from heaven, going,
'What God's made clean, don't you dare call unclean, dipstick.' This whole deal happened three times, and then it all got hauled back up into heaven, epic style. Right then, no lie, three guys show up at the door of the house where I'm crashing, sent all the way from Caesarea just for me. The Spirit told me to roll with 'em, no questions asked. These six brothers came along too, and we all piled into the guy's house.

web_text:
He told us how he had seen the angel standing in his house, and saying to him, ‘Send to Joppa, and get Simon, who is called Peter, who will speak to you words by which you will be saved, you and all your house.’ As I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell on them, even as on us at the beginning. I remembered the word of the Lord, how he said,
‘John indeed baptized in water, but you will be baptized in the Holy Spirit.’
slang_text:
He told us how he'd seen an angel just standing there in his house, telling him, 'Send some guys to Joppa and grab Simon, the one they call Peter -- he's gonna tell you stuff that'll get you saved, you and your whole household.' So I start talking, and boom, the Holy Spirit falls on 'em, same as it did on us right at the start. That's when I remembered what the Lord said,
'John dunked people in water, sure, but you're gonna get baptized in the Holy Spirit.'

web_text: If then God gave to them the same gift as us, when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I, that I could withstand God?” When they heard these things, they held their peace, and glorified God, saying, “Then God has also granted to the Gentiles repentance to life!” They therefore who were scattered abroad by the oppression that arose about Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, speaking the word to no one except to Jews only. But there were some of them, men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who, when they had come to Antioch, spoke to the Hellenists, preaching the Lord Jesus.
slang_text: So if God gave them the exact same gift he gave us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who am I to go toe-to-toe with God? As if!" When they heard that, they totally chilled out, kept their mouths shut, and just praised God, saying, "Whoa -- so God's handed the Gentiles a shot at repentance and life too!" Meanwhile, the folks who'd scattered outta town during the whole Stephen persecution mess traveled clear out to Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, but they were only spilling the word to Jews, nobody else. Except some of 'em, dudes from Cyprus and Cyrene, rolled into Antioch and started talking to the Hellenists too, preaching straight-up about the Lord Jesus.

web_text: The hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number believed and turned to the Lord. The report concerning them came to the ears of the assembly which was in Jerusalem. They sent out Barnabas to go as far as Antioch, who, when he had come, and had seen the grace of God, was glad. He exhorted them all, that with purpose of heart they should remain near to the Lord. For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Spirit and of faith, and many people were added to the Lord.
slang_text: The Lord's hand was totally on them, and a huge crowd believed and turned to the Lord, fer sure. Word of all this reached the ears of the church back in Jerusalem, and they sent Barnabas out to Antioch to check it out. When he got there and saw God's grace in action, he was stoked. He hyped everybody up to stick close to the Lord with their whole heart. Barnabas was a righteous dude, packed full of the Holy Spirit and faith, and a bodacious number of people got added to the Lord.

web_text: Barnabas went out to Tarsus to look for Saul. When he had found him, he brought him to Antioch. For a whole year they were gathered together with the assembly, and taught many people. The disciples were first called Christians in Antioch. Now in these days, prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch. One of them named Agabus stood up, and indicated by the Spirit that there should be a great famine all over the world, which also happened in the days of Claudius.
slang_text: Barnabas bounced out to Tarsus to track down Saul. Once he found him, he hauled him back to Antioch. For a whole year the two of 'em kicked it with the church there, teaching a ton of people. Antioch's where the disciples first got tagged "Christians" -- talk about a name that stuck. Around this time, some prophets rolled down from Jerusalem to Antioch, and one of 'em, a guy named Agabus, stood up and, through the Spirit, called it: there was gonna be a massive famine hitting the whole world. And sure enough, it went down just like he said, during Claudius's reign.

web_text: As any of the disciples had plenty, each determined to send relief to the brothers who lived in Judea; which they also did, sending it to the elders by the hands of Barnabas and Saul.
slang_text: So every disciple who had a little extra decided to send some relief to the brothers back in Judea. And they didn't just talk about it, either -- they made it happen like a boss, shipping it off to the elders via Barnabas and Saul.

web_text: Now about that time, King Herod stretched out his hands to oppress some of the assembly. He killed James, the brother of John, with the sword. When he saw that it pleased the Jews, he proceeded to seize Peter also. This was during the days of unleavened bread. When he had arrested him, he put him in prison, and delivered him to four squads of four soldiers each to guard him, intending to bring him out to the people after the Passover.
slang_text: Right around then, King Herod started coming down hard on the church, totally bogus move. He had James, John's brother, killed with the sword -- way harsh, no joke. When he saw that made the Jewish leaders happy, he figured he'd grab Peter too. This all went down during the days of unleavened bread. He got Peter locked up, tossed in prison, and had four squads of four soldiers each guarding him round the clock, planning to drag him out in front of everybody after Passover.

web_text: Peter therefore was kept in the prison, but constant prayer was made by the assembly to God for him. The same night when Herod was about to bring him out, Peter was sleeping between two soldiers, bound with two chains. Guards in front of the door kept the prison. And behold, an angel of the Lord stood by him, and a light shone in the cell. He struck Peter on the side, and woke him up, saying, “Stand up quickly!” His chains fell off from his hands. The angel said to him, “Get dressed and put on your sandals.” He did so. He said to him, “Put on your cloak, and follow me.”
slang_text: So Peter's stuck in the slammer, but the whole church is praying nonstop for him -- no chill pills, just straight-up prayer marathon. The very night before Herod's gonna bring him out, Peter's totally zonked out asleep between two soldiers, chained up with two chains, and guards posted right at the door. Then, oh snap, an angel of the Lord shows up out of nowhere and a light floods the whole cell. The angel whacks Peter on the side to wake him up, goes, "Get up, quick, dude!" And just like that, the chains fall right off his hands, no lie. The angel's like, "Get dressed, put your sandals on." Peter does it. Then, "Throw your cloak on and follow me."

web_text: And he went out and followed him. He didn’t know that what was being done by the angel was real, but thought he saw a vision. When they were past the first and the second guard, they came to the iron gate that leads into the city, which opened to them by itself. They went out, and went down one street, and immediately the angel departed from him. When Peter had come to himself, he said, “Now I truly know that the Lord has sent out his angel and delivered me out of the hand of Herod, and from everything the Jewish people were expecting.” Thinking about that, he came to the house of Mary, the mother of John who was called Mark, where many were gathered together and were praying.
slang_text: So Peter goes right along after him, but he's totally trippin' -- he figures this whole thing is just some vision, not for real. They cruise past the first guard, then the second, and reach the iron gate leading into the city, and boom, it swings open all by itself, no hands needed. They step out, walk down one street, and just like that the angel bounces, gone. Peter finally snaps out of it and goes, "Okay, now I know for real, no doubt -- the Lord sent his angel and busted me outta Herod's grip, and outta everything the Jewish leaders were cooking up." Once that sinks in, he heads over to Mary's place, mother of John also called Mark, where a whole crew was gathered praying their hearts out.

web_text: When Peter knocked at the door of the gate, a maid named Rhoda came to answer. When she recognized Peter’s voice, she didn’t open the gate for joy, but ran in, and reported that Peter was standing in front of the gate. They said to her, “You are crazy!” But she insisted that it was so. They said, “It is his angel.” But Peter continued knocking. When they had opened, they saw him, and were amazed.
slang_text: Peter knocks on the gate door, and this girl Rhoda comes to check who it is. Soon as she recognizes Peter's voice, she gets so hyped she totally forgets to open the gate -- just bolts back inside screaming that Peter's out there standing at the door. Everybody's like, "You're a total spaz, you're trippin'!" But she won't back down, swears up and down it's true. So they go, "Nah, that's gotta be his angel." Meanwhile Peter's out there still knocking away. Finally they open up, see him standing there, and about lose their minds, totally amazed.

web_text: But he, beckoning to them with his hand to be silent, declared to them how the Lord had brought him out of the prison. He said, “Tell these things to James, and to the brothers.” Then he departed, and went to another place. Now as soon as it was day, there was no small stir among the soldiers about what had become of Peter. When Herod had sought for him, and didn’t find him, he examined the guards, and commanded that they should be put to death. He went down from Judea to Caesarea, and stayed there. Now Herod was very angry with the people of Tyre and Sidon. They came with one accord to him, and, having made Blastus, the king’s personal aide, their friend, they asked for peace, because their country depended on the king’s country for food.
slang_text: Peter waves his hand for everybody to chill and pipe down, then lays out the whole story of how the Lord busted him outta prison. "Tell James and the brothers what went down," he says. Then he bounces, heads off somewhere else. Come morning, the soldiers are freaking out, no small deal, wondering what happened to Peter. Herod goes looking for him, can't find him anywhere, so he grills the guards and orders them executed -- way harsh. Then he heads down from Judea to Caesarea and hangs out there. Meanwhile Herod's hella ticked off at the folks from Tyre and Sidon. So they team up, get chummy with Blastus, the king's right-hand guy, and beg for peace, 'cause their whole country's food supply depended on the king's turf.

web_text: On an appointed day, Herod dressed himself in royal clothing, sat on the throne, and gave a speech to them. The people shouted, “The voice of a god, and not of a man!” Immediately an angel of the Lord struck him, because he didn’t give God the glory, and he was eaten by worms and died. But the word of God grew and multiplied.
slang_text: On the big day, Herod suits up in his royal robes, plants himself on the throne, and gives the crowd a whole speech. The people start hollering, "That's the voice of a god, not just some man!" And Herod eats it up, doesn't give God the glory -- big mistake. Immediately an angel of the Lord strikes him down, and it's grim: worms eat him alive till he dies, no joke, that's some grody, way harsh judgment. But God's word just kept growing and spreading like crazy.

web_text: Barnabas and Saul returned to Jerusalem, when they had fulfilled their service, also taking with them John who was called Mark.
slang_text: Barnabas and Saul finished up what they came to do and headed back to Jerusalem, bringing John, also called Mark, along for the ride.

web_text: Now in the assembly that was at Antioch there were some prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon who was called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen the foster brother of Herod the tetrarch, and Saul. As they served the Lord and fasted, the Holy Spirit said, “Separate Barnabas and Saul for me, for the work to which I have called them.” Then, when they had fasted and prayed and laid their hands on them, they sent them away. So, being sent out by the Holy Spirit, they went down to Seleucia. From there they sailed to Cyprus.
slang_text: So there's this radical crew of prophets and teachers hangin' at the Antioch assembly: Barnabas, that Simeon dude they called Niger, Lucius from Cyrene, Manaen (who grew up practically as Herod the tetrarch's bro), and Saul. While they were all serving the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit busts in with, "Yo, set apart Barnabas and Saul for me -- I got a job for these two, for real." So after fasting and praying and laying hands on 'em, they sent 'em off. Totally sent out by the Holy Spirit, they bounced down to Seleucia, then set sail for Cyprus.

web_text: When they were at Salamis, they proclaimed God’s word in the Jewish synagogues. They had also John as their attendant. When they had gone through the island to Paphos, they found a certain sorcerer, a false prophet, a Jew, whose name was Bar Jesus, who was with the proconsul, Sergius Paulus, a man of understanding. This man summoned Barnabas and Saul, and sought to hear the word of God. But Elymas the sorcerer (for so is his name by interpretation) withstood them, seeking to turn the proconsul away from the faith.
slang_text: When they hit Salamis, they were droppin' God's word all over the Jewish synagogues, with John tagging along as their helper. They cruised the whole island to Paphos and ran into this grody sorcerer -- a total fake prophet, a Jew named Bar Jesus, kickin' it with the proconsul Sergius Paulus, who was actually a pretty sharp dude. This proconsul called Barnabas and Paul over 'cause he wanted the lowdown on God's word. But Elymas the sorcerer (that's what his name means, translated) tried to mess it all up, working overtime to keep the proconsul from believing.

web_text:
But Saul, who is also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, fastened his eyes on him, and said, “Full of all deceit and all cunning, you son of the devil, you enemy of all righteousness, will you not cease to pervert the right ways of the Lord? Now, behold, the hand of the Lord is on you, and you will be blind, not seeing the sun for a season!”
Immediately a mist and darkness fell on him. He went around seeking someone to lead him by the hand. Then the proconsul, when he saw what was done, believed, being astonished at the teaching of the Lord.
slang_text:
But Saul -- also known as Paul -- totally jacked up with the Holy Spirit, locked eyes on the guy and let him have it: "You bogus fraud, full of every trick in the book, you spawn of the devil, enemy of everything righteous -- you gonna quit twisting up the Lord's straight paths or what? Check it: the Lord's hand is on you right now, and you're goin' blind, no sun for you for a while!"

Boom -- a fog and darkness dropped on him instantly. Dude was stumbling around trying to find somebody to grab his hand and lead him. When the proconsul saw that go down, he believed, totally blown away by the teaching about the Lord.

web_text: Now Paul and his company set sail from Paphos, and came to Perga in Pamphylia. John departed from them and returned to Jerusalem. But they, passing on from Perga, came to Antioch of Pisidia. They went into the synagogue on the Sabbath day, and sat down. After the reading of the law and the prophets, the rulers of the synagogue sent to them, saying, “Brothers, if you have any word of exhortation for the people, speak.” Paul stood up, and beckoning with his hand said, “Men of Israel, and you who fear God, listen.
slang_text: So Paul and his crew set sail outta Paphos and landed at Perga in Pamphylia. John bailed on 'em there and bounced back to Jerusalem. But the rest kept truckin' from Perga on to Antioch of Pisidia. They rolled into the synagogue on the Sabbath and took a seat. After they read through the law and the prophets, the synagogue rulers sent word over: "Brothers, if you got any word of encouragement for these people, lay it on us." Paul stood up, waved his hand for quiet, and said, "Men of Israel, and all you God-fearing folks, listen up.

web_text: The God of this people chose our fathers, and exalted the people when they stayed as aliens in the land of Egypt, and with an uplifted arm, he led them out of it. For a period of about forty years he put up with them in the wilderness. When he had destroyed seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave them their land for an inheritance, for about four hundred fifty years. After these things he gave them judges until Samuel the prophet.
slang_text: The God of this people totally picked our ancestors and made 'em big-time when they were stuck as strangers in Egypt, and with a mighty arm he busted 'em outta there. For like forty years he put up with 'em out in the wilderness -- no small feat. After he wiped out seven nations in Canaan, he handed 'em the land as their own, all told about four hundred fifty years. After that he set 'em up with judges, all the way till Samuel the prophet showed up.

web_text: Afterward they asked for a king, and God gave to them Saul the son of Kish, a man of the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years. When he had removed him, he raised up David to be their king, to whom he also testified, ‘I have found David the son of Jesse, a man after my heart, who will do all my will.’ From this man’s offspring, God has brought salvation to Israel according to his promise, before his coming, when John had first preached the baptism of repentance to Israel.
slang_text: Later on they wanted a king, so God hooked 'em up with Saul, son of Kish, a dude from the tribe of Benjamin, for forty years. When God bounced him, he raised up David to be their king, and even gave him props, sayin', 'I found David, son of Jesse, a guy after my own heart, gonna do everything I want.' From this guy's family line, God brought salvation to Israel, just like he promised, and right before it all went down, John was out there preaching a baptism of turning-your-life-around to Israel.

web_text: As John was fulfilling his course, he said, ‘What do you suppose that I am? I am not he. But behold, one comes after me, the sandals of whose feet I am not worthy to untie.’ Brothers, children of the stock of Abraham, and those among you who fear God, the word of this salvation is sent out to you. For those who dwell in Jerusalem, and their rulers, because they didn’t know him, nor the voices of the prophets which are read every Sabbath, fulfilled them by condemning him. Though they found no cause for death, they still asked Pilate to have him killed.
slang_text: While John was finishing up his gig, he said, 'What do you dudes think I am? I'm not the one. No way -- but somebody's comin' after me, and I'm not even worthy to untie his sandals.' Brothers, sons of Abraham's family, and all you God-fearing folks here, this message of salvation got sent straight to you. See, the people livin' in Jerusalem and their rulers, they didn't have a clue who he was, didn't get the prophets' words that get read every single Sabbath -- so they fulfilled it all by condemning him. Even though they couldn't pin a death-worthy charge on him, they still begged Pilate to have him killed.

web_text: When they had fulfilled all things that were written about him, they took him down from the tree, and laid him in a tomb. But God raised him from the dead, and he was seen for many days by those who came up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem, who are his witnesses to the people. We bring you good news of the promise made to the fathers,
slang_text: When they'd done everything written about him, they took him down off the tree and laid him in a tomb. But God raised him up from the dead, for real, and he was seen for days and days by the guys who'd traveled with him from Galilee to Jerusalem -- they're his witnesses to the people now. And we're here bringing you the good news of the promise made to our ancestors,

web_text:
that God has fulfilled the same to us, their children, in that he raised up Jesus. As it is also written in the second psalm,
‘You are my Son.
Today I have become your father.’ “Concerning that he raised him up from the dead, now no more to return to corruption, he has spoken thus: ‘I will give you the holy and sure blessings of David.’ Therefore he says also in another psalm, ‘You will not allow your Holy One to see decay.’ For David, after he had in his own generation served the counsel of God, fell asleep, and was laid with his fathers, and saw decay.
slang_text:
that God totally made good on it for us, their kids, by raising up Jesus. Like it's written in the second psalm,
'You are my Son.
Today I've become your father.' Concerning how he raised him from the dead, never to rot again, God spoke it plain: 'I'll give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David.' That's why it says in another psalm too, 'You won't let your Holy One see decay.' 'Cause David, after he served God's plan in his own generation, fell asleep, got laid with his ancestors, and yeah, he did see decay.

web_text: But he whom God raised up saw no decay. Be it known to you therefore, brothers, that through this man is proclaimed to you remission of sins, and by him everyone who believes is justified from all things, from which you could not be justified by the law of Moses. Beware therefore, lest that come on you which is spoken in the prophets:
slang_text: But the one God raised up never saw decay, no how. So get this straight, brothers: through this man, forgiveness of sins is bein' preached to you, and everybody who believes in him is made right with God from all the stuff the law of Moses couldn't clear you from. So watch out, don't let what the prophets said happen to you:

web_text:
But when the Jews saw the multitudes, they were filled with jealousy, and contradicted the things which were spoken by Paul, and blasphemed. Paul and Barnabas spoke out boldly, and said, “It was necessary that God’s word should be spoken to you first. Since indeed you thrust it from you, and judge yourselves unworthy of eternal life, behold, we turn to the Gentiles. For so has the Lord commanded us, saying,
‘I have set you as a light for the Gentiles,
that you should bring salvation to the uttermost parts of the earth.’” As the Gentiles heard this, they were glad, and glorified the word of God. As many as were appointed to eternal life believed.
slang_text:
But when the Jews saw the massive crowds, they got straight-up jealous, started dissin' everything Paul was sayin' and even blaspheming. Paul and Barnabas didn't back down one bit and said, "It had to be God's word came to you dudes first. But since you're shovin' it away and judgin' yourselves not worth eternal life, well -- check it, we're turnin' to the Gentiles now. 'Cause the Lord straight up commanded us this:
'I've set you as a light for the Gentiles,
so you'd carry salvation clear to the ends of the earth.'" When the Gentiles heard that, they were stoked, totally praising God's word, and every last one marked out for eternal life believed.

web_text: The Lord’s word was spread abroad throughout all the region. But the Jews stirred up the devout and prominent women and the chief men of the city, and stirred up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and threw them out of their borders. But they shook off the dust of their feet against them, and came to Iconium. The disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.
slang_text: The Lord's word spread out through that whole region like wildfire. But the Jews stirred up the devout, high-society women and the top dudes of the city, whipped up a persecution against Paul and Barnabas, and ran 'em clean outta town. So they just shook the dust off their feet at 'em and bounced to Iconium. Meanwhile the disciples were kickin' it totally full of joy and the Holy Spirit.

web_text: In Iconium, they entered together into the synagogue of the Jews, and so spoke that a great multitude both of Jews and of Greeks believed. But the disbelieving Jews stirred up and embittered the souls of the Gentiles against the brothers. Therefore they stayed there a long time, speaking boldly in the Lord, who testified to the word of his grace, granting signs and wonders to be done by their hands. But the multitude of the city was divided. Part sided with the Jews, and part with the apostles.
slang_text: Over in Iconium, Paul and Barnabas rolled into the synagogue together, and they preached so bodaciously that a huge crowd of Jews and Greeks totally believed. But the Jews who wouldn't buy it started stirring up bad vibes, poisoning the Gentiles against the brothers. Still, the dudes stuck around a long time, speaking bold in the Lord, who backed up their whole message of grace by letting 'em pull off signs and wonders with their own hands. But the city split right down the middle -- some sided with the Jews, some sided with the apostles. Total chaos.

web_text: When some of both the Gentiles and the Jews, with their rulers, made a violent attempt to mistreat and stone them, they became aware of it, and fled to the cities of Lycaonia, Lystra, Derbe, and the surrounding region. There they preached the Good News. At Lystra a certain man sat, impotent in his feet, a cripple from his mother’s womb, who never had walked.
slang_text: Then some Gentiles and Jews, plus the big shots running things, cooked up a way harsh plot to rough 'em up and stone 'em. Paul and Barnabas caught wind of it and bounced outta there, high-tailing it to Lystra, Derbe, and the towns around Lycaonia. They kept preaching the Good News wherever they landed. Now in Lystra there was this guy sitting around who couldn't use his feet -- born a cripple, dude had never walked a step in his whole life.

web_text: He was listening to Paul speaking, who, fastening eyes on him, and seeing that he had faith to be made whole, said with a loud voice, “Stand upright on your feet!” He leaped up and walked. When the multitude saw what Paul had done, they lifted up their voice, saying in the language of Lycaonia, “The gods have come down to us in the likeness of men!” They called Barnabas “Jupiter”, and Paul “Mercury”, because he was the chief speaker.
slang_text: This guy was tuned in while Paul was preaching, and Paul locked eyes on him, saw he had the faith to totally be healed, and hollered out, "Get up on your feet!" The dude leaped up and started walking, no duh it was a miracle. When the crowd saw what Paul pulled off, they lost their minds, shouting in their own Lycaonian tongue, "The gods have come down here disguised as regular dudes!" They started calling Barnabas "Jupiter" and Paul "Mercury," 'cause he did most of the talking.

web_text: The priest of Jupiter, whose temple was in front of their city, brought oxen and garlands to the gates, and would have made a sacrifice along with the multitudes. But when the apostles, Barnabas and Paul, heard of it, they tore their clothes, and sprang into the multitude, crying out, “Men, why are you doing these things? We also are men of like passions with you, and bring you good news, that you should turn from these vain things to the living God, who made the sky, the earth, the sea, and all that is in them; who in the generations gone by allowed all the nations to walk in their own ways.
slang_text: The priest from Jupiter's temple, which sat right at the front of the city, dragged out oxen and flower garlands to the gates, all set to throw a sacrifice with the whole crowd. But when Barnabas and Paul got word of it, they ripped their own clothes in horror and dove into the mob, yelling, "Dudes, what's your damage? Why are you doing this? We're just regular guys like you, same feelings and everything! We're here to bring you good news -- turn away from this bogus stuff and get right with the living God, the one who made the sky, the earth, the sea, and everything in 'em. Back in the day he let every nation go their own way, sure --"

web_text: Yet he didn’t leave himself without witness, in that he did good and gave you rains from the sky and fruitful seasons, filling our hearts with food and gladness.” Even saying these things, they hardly stopped the multitudes from making a sacrifice to them. But some Jews from Antioch and Iconium came there, and having persuaded the multitudes, they stoned Paul, and dragged him out of the city, supposing that he was dead. But as the disciples stood around him, he rose up, and entered into the city. On the next day he went out with Barnabas to Derbe.
slang_text: -- "but he never left himself without a witness, seeing how he's been doing you good the whole time, sending rain from the sky and good harvests, filling your hearts with grub and gladness." Even after all that, they could barely stop the crowd from sacrificing to 'em, as if. But then some Jews rolled in from Antioch and Iconium, talked the crowd into it, and stoned Paul, dragging him outside the city 'cause they figured he was dead as a doornail. But the disciples gathered around him, and he just got up and walked back into the city. Talk about resilient. Next day he took off with Barnabas for Derbe.

web_text: When they had preached the Good News to that city, and had made many disciples, they returned to Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch, confirming the souls of the disciples, exhorting them to continue in the faith, and that through many afflictions we must enter into God’s Kingdom. When they had appointed elders for them in every assembly, and had prayed with fasting, they commended them to the Lord, on whom they had believed. They passed through Pisidia, and came to Pamphylia.
slang_text: After they preached the Good News in that city and racked up a bunch of disciples, they doubled back through Lystra, Iconium, and Antioch, pumping up the disciples' souls and telling 'em straight up: keep the faith, no matter what, 'cause we gotta go through a ton of rough patches to get into God's Kingdom -- that's just how it is. They picked out elders for each church, prayed with fasting over 'em, and handed 'em off to the Lord they'd put their trust in. Then they cut through Pisidia and landed in Pamphylia.

web_text: When they had spoken the word in Perga, they went down to Attalia. From there they sailed to Antioch, from where they had been committed to the grace of God for the work which they had fulfilled. When they had arrived, and had gathered the assembly together, they reported all the things that God had done with them, and that he had opened a door of faith to the nations. They stayed there with the disciples for a long time.
slang_text: After dropping the word in Perga, they headed down to Attalia. From there they set sail back to Antioch, the same spot where they'd first gotten sent off into God's grace for the job they'd just finished, like a boss. Once they got there, they called the whole church together and gave 'em the full report on everything God had pulled off through them, and how he'd totally cracked open a door of faith for the nations. Then they kicked it there with the disciples for a good long while.

web_text: Some men came down from Judea and taught the brothers, “Unless you are circumcised after the custom of Moses, you can’t be saved.” Therefore when Paul and Barnabas had no small discord and discussion with them, they appointed Paul and Barnabas, and some others of them, to go up to Jerusalem to the apostles and elders about this question. They, being sent on their way by the assembly, passed through both Phoenicia and Samaria, declaring the conversion of the Gentiles. They caused great joy to all the brothers. When they had come to Jerusalem, they were received by the assembly and the apostles and the elders, and they reported all things that God had done with them.
slang_text: So check it -- some dudes roll down from Judea and start preaching to the brothers, "Unless you get circumcised the old-school Moses way, forget it, you can't be saved, no way." Paul and Barnabas were not about to let that slide -- major beef broke out, big-time arguing. So the church decided Paul, Barnabas, and a few other guys should book it up to Jerusalem and hash this whole thing out with the apostles and elders. The church sent them off, and on the way they cruised through Phoenicia and Samaria, spreading the word that the Gentiles were getting saved -- totally made everybody stoked. When they finally hit Jerusalem, the church, the apostles, and the elders welcomed them in, and Paul and Barnabas laid out everything righteous God had pulled off through them.

web_text: But some of the sect of the Pharisees who believed rose up, saying, “It is necessary to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses.” The apostles and the elders were gathered together to see about this matter. When there had been much discussion, Peter rose up and said to them, “Brothers, you know that a good while ago God made a choice among you, that by my mouth the nations should hear the word of the Good News, and believe. God, who knows the heart, testified about them, giving them the Holy Spirit, just like he did to us.
slang_text: But then some Pharisee dudes who'd come to believe stood up and dropped a bogus demand: "Gotta circumcise 'em, and make 'em follow the whole law of Moses, no excuses." So the apostles and elders huddled up to sort out this mess. After a ton of back-and-forth, Peter got up and said, "Listen, brothers -- y'all know way back when, God picked me outta the crew so the Gentiles would hear the Good News straight from my mouth and believe it, fer sure. And God, who sees straight into everybody's heart, backed 'em up -- he gave 'em the Holy Spirit, same as he gave us. No duh he's on board with this."

web_text:
He made no distinction between us and them, cleansing their hearts by faith. Now therefore why do you tempt God, that you should put a yoke on the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear? But we believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus,
 just as they are.” All the multitude kept silence, and they listened to Barnabas and Paul reporting what signs and wonders God had done among the nations through them.
slang_text: "He didn't play favorites between us and them -- he scrubbed their hearts clean by faith, same deal. So why you trippin' and testing God, trying to strap a yoke on these disciples' necks that neither our own ancestors nor us could ever handle? Come on, we believe we get saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, and that's the fer-real deal for them too, just like us." That shut the whole crowd right up -- everybody chilled out and listened while Barnabas and Paul told about all the epic signs and wonders God pulled off among the Gentiles through them.

web_text:
After they were silent, James answered, “Brothers, listen to me. Simeon has reported how God first visited the nations, to take out of them a people for his name. This agrees with the words of the prophets. As it is written, ‘After these things I will return.
I will again build the tabernacle of David, which has fallen.
I will again build its ruins.
I will set it up,
slang_text:
Once everybody chilled out, James spoke up: "Brothers, listen up. Simeon just told us how God first swung by to grab himself a people for his name outta the Gentiles -- and that's totally lined up with what the prophets said. Like it's written, 'After this stuff goes down, I'm coming back.
I'm gonna rebuild David's tent that fell apart.
Gonna fix up all its ruins.
Gonna set it back up,

so the rest of humanity can go looking for the Lord --
every Gentile called by my name,
says the Lord, and he's the one making all this happen.' God's had this planned since forever, no big surprise to him. So here's my call: let's not hassle the Gentiles who are turning to God with a bunch of bogus rules. Instead let's just write and tell 'em to steer clear of stuff polluted by idols, sexual immorality, meat from strangled animals, and blood.

web_text:
For Moses from generations of old has in every city those who preach him, being read in the synagogues every Sabbath.” Then it seemed good to the apostles and the elders, with the whole assembly, to choose men out of their company, and send them to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas: Judas called Barsabbas, and Silas, chief men among the brothers. They wrote these things by their hand:
“The apostles, the elders, and the brothers, to the brothers who are of the Gentiles in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: greetings. Because we have heard that some who went out from us have troubled you with words, unsettling your souls, saying, ‘You must be circumcised and keep the law,’ to whom we gave no commandment;
slang_text:
"'Cause Moses has had guys preaching him in every city forever, read out loud in the synagogues every single Sabbath -- so this'll be no surprise to anybody." So the apostles, the elders, and the whole church figured it was righteous to pick some guys from their own crew and send 'em off to Antioch with Paul and Barnabas: Judas, also called Barsabbas, and Silas, both leaders among the brothers. They wrote this letter and had it hand-delivered:

"From the apostles, the elders, and the brothers, to the Gentile brothers in Antioch, Syria, and Cilicia: what's up. We heard some dudes came from our crew and stirred up bogus trouble, messing with your heads, saying, 'You gotta get circumcised and keep the whole law' -- but we never told 'em to say that, no way, talk to the hand on that one.

web_text: it seemed good to us, having come to one accord, to choose out men and send them to you with our beloved Barnabas and Paul, men who have risked their lives for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. We have sent therefore Judas and Silas, who themselves will also tell you the same things by word of mouth. For it seemed good to the Holy Spirit, and to us, to lay no greater burden on you than these necessary things:
slang_text: So we all got together and agreed it was righteous to pick some guys and send 'em your way along with our totally beloved Barnabas and Paul -- dudes who've straight-up risked their necks for the name of our Lord Jesus Christ. We're sending Judas and Silas, and they'll tell you the same stuff in person, word for word. See, it seemed choice to the Holy Spirit, and to us too, not to dump any heavier load on you than these must-haves:

web_text: that you abstain from things sacrificed to idols, from blood, from things strangled, and from sexual immorality, from which if you keep yourselves, it will be well with you. Farewell.” So, when they were sent off, they came to Antioch. Having gathered the multitude together, they delivered the letter. When they had read it, they rejoiced over the encouragement. Judas and Silas, also being prophets themselves, encouraged the brothers with many words, and strengthened them.
slang_text: steer clear of stuff sacrificed to idols, blood, meat from strangled animals, and sexual immorality. Keep away from that stuff and you'll be solid. Peace out." So they got sent off and rolled into Antioch, gathered the whole crowd together, and handed over the letter. When it got read out loud, everybody was totally stoked over the encouragement in it. Judas and Silas, being prophets themselves, hyped up the brothers with a ton of righteous words and got 'em pumped up strong.

web_text: After they had spent some time there, they were sent back with greetings from the brothers to the apostles.  But Paul and Barnabas stayed in Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, with many others also. After some days Paul said to Barnabas, “Let’s return now and visit our brothers in every city in which we proclaimed the word of the Lord, to see how they are doing.”
slang_text: After hanging there a while, they got sent back with the brothers' blessing to the apostles. But Paul and Barnabas stuck around Antioch, teaching and preaching the word of the Lord, plus a bunch of other folks were at it too. After a few days Paul goes to Barnabas, "Let's bounce and go check on our brothers in every city where we dropped the word of the Lord, see how they're holding up."

web_text: Barnabas planned to take John, who was called Mark, with them also. But Paul didn’t think that it was a good idea to take with them someone who had withdrawn from them in Pamphylia, and didn’t go with them to do the work. Then the contention grew so sharp that they separated from each other. Barnabas took Mark with him, and sailed away to Cyprus, but Paul chose Silas, and went out, being commended by the brothers to the grace of God.
slang_text: Barnabas was all for bringing John, the guy called Mark, along too. But Paul was totally against it -- he figured it was a bogus idea to drag along somebody who'd bailed on 'em back in Pamphylia and didn't stick around to finish the work. Things got way harsh between 'em, the argument got so sharp they flat-out split up. Barnabas took Mark and sailed off to Cyprus, while Paul picked Silas and headed out, with the brothers commending him to God's grace. Straight-up bummer, watching two tight friends come to that.

web_text: He went through Syria and Cilicia, strengthening the assemblies.
slang_text: He cruised on through Syria and Cilicia, pumping up the churches and keeping 'em strong.

web_text: He came to Derbe and Lystra: and behold, a certain disciple was there, named Timothy, the son of a Jewess who believed; but his father was a Greek. The brothers who were at Lystra and Iconium gave a good testimony about him. Paul wanted to have him go out with him, and he took and circumcised him because of the Jews who were in those parts; for they all knew that his father was a Greek. As they went on their way through the cities, they delivered the decrees to them to keep which had been ordained by the apostles and elders who were at Jerusalem.
slang_text: Paul rolled into Derbe and Lystra, and check it, there was this disciple named Timothy hanging out there, son of a Jewish believer mom and a Greek dad. The brothers over in Lystra and Iconium gave him a totally righteous testimony, so Paul wanted the kid to bounce along with him. Paul went ahead and got him circumcised though, on account of the Jews in that area -- everybody knew his dad was Greek, and Paul didn't want that to be a hangup. As they made their way through the cities, they delivered the decrees the apostles and elders back in Jerusalem had laid down, so the folks could keep 'em, like a boss.

web_text: So the assemblies were strengthened in the faith, and increased in number daily. When they had gone through the region of Phrygia and Galatia, they were forbidden by the Holy Spirit to speak the word in Asia. When they had come opposite Mysia, they tried to go into Bithynia, but the Spirit didn’t allow them. Passing by Mysia, they came down to Troas.
slang_text: So the assemblies got totally strengthened in the faith, and more people kept joining up every single day, fer sure. They cruised through Phrygia and Galatia, but the Holy Spirit straight up told 'em, no way, don't preach the word in Asia. When they got up near Mysia they tried to jet into Bithynia, but the Spirit wasn't havin' it. So they cut past Mysia and rolled down to Troas instead.

web_text: A vision appeared to Paul in the night. There was a man of Macedonia standing, begging him, and saying, “Come over into Macedonia and help us.” When he had seen the vision, immediately we sought to go out to Macedonia, concluding that the Lord had called us to preach the Good News to them. Setting sail therefore from Troas, we made a straight course to Samothrace, and the day following to Neapolis; and from there to Philippi, which is a city of Macedonia, the foremost of the district, a Roman colony. We were staying some days in this city.
slang_text: That night Paul got this gnarly vision -- a dude from Macedonia standing there, begging him, "Come on over to Macedonia and help us out!" Soon as Paul saw it, we were like, no doubt, we're outta here, figuring the Lord had totally called us to bring 'em the Good News. So we set sail from Troas, cruised straight to Samothrace, then Neapolis the next day, then on to Philippi, the top city in that district of Macedonia and a Roman colony to boot. We chilled there for a few days.

web_text: On the Sabbath day we went outside of the city by a riverside, where we supposed there was a place of prayer, and we sat down, and spoke to the women who had come together. A certain woman named Lydia, a seller of purple, of the city of Thyatira, one who worshiped God, heard us; whose heart the Lord opened to listen to the things which were spoken by Paul. When she and her household were baptized, she begged us, saying, “If you have judged me to be faithful to the Lord, come into my house, and stay.” So she persuaded us. As we were going to prayer, a certain girl having a spirit of divination met us, who brought her masters much gain by fortune telling.
slang_text: On the Sabbath we headed outside the city, down by the river, where we figured folks gathered for prayer. We plopped down and started talking to the women who'd shown up. One Betty named Lydia was there, a purple-cloth dealer from Thyatira, a total God-worshiper who was listening in -- and the Lord opened her heart right up to soak in what Paul was saying. She got baptized along with her whole household, then begged us, "If y'all figure I'm faithful to the Lord, come crash at my place and stay a while." She wouldn't take no for an answer, so we went. Then, headed to prayer one day, we ran into this slave girl with a spirit of fortune-telling in her -- she was rakin' in mad cash for her masters with that gig.

web_text:
Following Paul and us, she cried out, “These men are servants of the Most High God, who proclaim to us a way of salvation!” She was doing this for many days.
But Paul, becoming greatly annoyed, turned and said to the spirit, “I command you in the name of Jesus Christ to come out of her!” It came out that very hour. But when her masters saw that the hope of their gain was gone, they seized Paul and Silas, and dragged them into the marketplace before the rulers. When they had brought them to the magistrates, they said, “These men, being Jews, are agitating our city,
slang_text: She started tailing Paul and the rest of us, screeching, "These dudes are servants of the Most High God, and they're layin' down the way to salvation for you!" She kept it up for days, way harsh and annoying. Paul finally got fed up -- I mean totally over it -- spun around and told the spirit, "In the name of Jesus Christ, I'm commanding you, get outta her!" And bam, it split that very hour. But when her masters saw their cash cow had dried up, they grabbed Paul and Silas and hauled 'em into the marketplace before the rulers. They dragged 'em in front of the magistrates and said, "These guys are Jews, and they're stirring up bogus trouble in our city,

web_text: and advocate customs which it is not lawful for us to accept or to observe, being Romans.” The multitude rose up together against them, and the magistrates tore their clothes from them, and commanded them to be beaten with rods. When they had laid many stripes on them, they threw them into prison, charging the jailer to keep them safely, who, having received such a command, threw them into the inner prison, and secured their feet in the stocks.
slang_text: and they're pushing customs that us Romans got no business messing with!" The crowd piled on against Paul and Silas, and the magistrates ripped their clothes right off 'em and ordered 'em beaten with rods. After they'd laid a ton of stripes on 'em, they tossed 'em in prison, telling the jailer to keep 'em locked up tight. So the jailer, taking that order dead serious, chucked 'em in the inner cell and clamped their feet in the stocks. Bogus treatment, big time.

web_text: But about midnight Paul and Silas were praying and singing hymns to God, and the prisoners were listening to them. Suddenly there was a great earthquake, so that the foundations of the prison were shaken; and immediately all the doors were opened, and everyone’s bonds were loosened. The jailer, being roused out of sleep and seeing the prison doors open, drew his sword and was about to kill himself, supposing that the prisoners had escaped. But Paul cried with a loud voice, saying, “Don’t harm yourself, for we are all here!”
slang_text: But around midnight, Paul and Silas were praying and belting out hymns to God, and the other prisoners were totally locked in, listening. Then outta nowhere, a massive earthquake hit -- the whole prison shook down to its foundations, every door flew open, and everybody's chains just fell loose. The jailer jolted awake, saw the doors busted open, and about to off himself with his sword, figuring all the prisoners had bounced. But Paul hollered out at the top of his lungs, "Don't you dare hurt yourself, dude -- we're all still here!"

web_text: He called for lights, sprang in, fell down trembling before Paul and Silas, brought them out, and said, “Sirs, what must I do to be saved?” They said, “Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ, and you will be saved, you and your household.” They spoke the word of the Lord to him, and to all who were in his house.
slang_text: The jailer called for some lights, rushed in, and fell down shaking in front of Paul and Silas. He brought 'em out and asked, dead serious, no joke in his voice at all, "Sirs, what do I gotta do to be saved?" They told him, "Believe in the Lord Jesus Christ, and you'll be saved -- you and your whole household." Then they spoke the word of the Lord to him and to everybody else in his house.

web_text: He took them the same hour of the night, and washed their stripes, and was immediately baptized, he and all his household. He brought them up into his house, and set food before them, and rejoiced greatly, with all his household, having believed in God. But when it was day, the magistrates sent the sergeants, saying, “Let those men go.” The jailer reported these words to Paul, saying, “The magistrates have sent to let you go; now therefore come out, and go in peace.”
slang_text: That same hour, in the dead of night, the jailer took 'em and washed up their wounds, and right then and there got baptized, him and his whole household. He brought 'em up to his house, laid out some food, and was totally stoked, celebrating with his whole family now that they'd believed in God. Come morning, the magistrates sent word through the officers, "Let those guys go." So the jailer told Paul, "The magistrates say you're free -- come on out and go in peace."

web_text: But Paul said to them, “They have beaten us publicly, without a trial, men who are Romans, and have cast us into prison! Do they now release us secretly? No, most certainly, but let them come themselves and bring us out!” The sergeants reported these words to the magistrates, and they were afraid when they heard that they were Romans, and they came and begged them. When they had brought them out, they asked them to depart from the city. They went out of the prison, and entered into Lydia’s house. When they had seen the brothers, they encouraged them, and departed.
slang_text: But Paul wasn't havin' it. "They beat us in public, no trial, no nothin', and we're Roman citizens! And now they wanna sneak us out on the down-low? As if! No way, dude -- they can drag themselves down here and walk us out personally." The officers reported all this back to the magistrates, and they totally freaked when they heard "Roman citizens" -- came running and begging Paul and Silas to chill. They walked 'em out, asked 'em nicely to just leave town already. So Paul and Silas left the prison, swung by Lydia's place, hyped up the brothers there, and then bounced.

web_text: Now when they had passed through Amphipolis and Apollonia, they came to Thessalonica, where there was a Jewish synagogue. Paul, as was his custom, went in to them, and for three Sabbath days reasoned with them from the Scriptures, explaining and demonstrating that the Christ had to suffer and rise again from the dead, and saying, “This Jesus, whom I proclaim to you, is the Christ.” Some of them were persuaded, and joined Paul and Silas, of the devout Greeks a great multitude, and not a few of the chief women.
slang_text: So Paul and the crew cruised through Amphipolis and Apollonia and rolled into Thessalonica, which had a synagogue and everything. Paul, doing his usual thing, went in and for three Sabbaths straight up broke it down from the Scriptures, laying out totally for real proof that the Christ had to suffer and then bounce back from the dead, telling 'em, "This Jesus I'm preaching on -- he's the Christ, no lie." A bunch of 'em got convinced and teamed up with Paul and Silas, plus a hella big crowd of devout Greeks and a good number of the top Betties in town.

web_text:
But the unpersuaded Jews took along some wicked men from the marketplace, and gathering a crowd, set the city in an uproar. Assaulting the house of Jason, they sought to bring them out to the people. When they didn’t find them, they dragged Jason and certain brothers
 before the rulers of the city, crying, “These who have turned the world upside down have come here also, whom Jason has received. These all act contrary to the decrees of Caesar, saying that there is another king, Jesus!” The multitude and the rulers of the city were troubled when they heard these things.
slang_text: But the Jews who weren't buying it were straight bogus about it -- they rounded up some grody dudes hanging around the marketplace, whipped up a mob, and set the whole city into an uproar. They stormed Jason's house trying to drag Paul and Silas out to the crowd. When they came up empty, they hauled Jason and some of the brothers before the city rulers instead, hollering, "These guys have been flipping the whole world upside down, and now they've rolled in here too, and Jason's been letting 'em crash at his place! They're all trippin' against Caesar's decrees, saying there's some other king named Jesus!" That got the crowd and the rulers all shook up, for real.

web_text: When they had taken security from Jason and the rest, they let them go. The brothers immediately sent Paul and Silas away by night to Beroea. When they arrived, they went into the Jewish synagogue. Now these were more noble than those in Thessalonica, in that they received the word with all readiness of mind, examining the Scriptures daily to see whether these things were so. Many of them therefore believed; also of the prominent Greek women, and not a few men.
slang_text: After the rulers got some bail money outta Jason and the rest, they let 'em go. The brothers wasted no time and sent Paul and Silas bouncing outta there that same night, off to Beroea. Once they got there, they hit up the synagogue same as always. Now these Bereans were way more noble than the Thessalonica crowd -- they took the message with total open minds, checking the Scriptures every single day to see if it was really fer sure legit. So a bunch of 'em believed, including a good crew of prominent Greek Betties and no small number of dudes too.

web_text: But when the Jews of Thessalonica had knowledge that the word of God was proclaimed by Paul at Beroea also, they came there likewise, agitating the multitudes. Then the brothers immediately sent out Paul to go as far as to the sea, and Silas and Timothy still stayed there. But those who escorted Paul brought him as far as Athens. Receiving a commandment to Silas and Timothy that they should come to him very quickly, they departed. Now while Paul waited for them at Athens, his spirit was provoked within him as he saw the city full of idols.
slang_text: But when the Thessalonica Jews got wind that Paul was preaching God's word in Beroea too, they came busting in there also, stirring up the crowds like the wastoids they were. So the brothers hustled Paul out right away, sending him toward the coast, while Silas and Timothy chilled behind. The guys escorting Paul walked him all the way to Athens, then bounced back with word for Silas and Timothy to catch up to him ASAP. As the sailors were trying to flee out of the ship, and had lowered the boat into the sea, pretending that they would lay out anchors from the bow, Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, “Unless these stay in the ship, you can’t be saved.” Then the soldiers cut away the ropes of the boat, and let it fall off.
slang_text: Scared stiff we'd smash into some rocks, they dropped four anchors off the stern and just prayed for daylight to hurry up already. Then the sailors tried to pull a straight-up bogus move -- lowering the lifeboat into the water like they were gonna lay out more anchors off the bow, when really they were just trying to bail and save their own skins. Paul caught it and told the centurion and the soldiers, "Unless these guys stay on this ship, there's no way any of you are gettin' saved." So the soldiers cut the ropes on the boat and let it just drop away.

web_text: While the day was coming on, Paul begged them all to take some food, saying, “Today is the fourteenth day that you wait and continue fasting, having taken nothing. Therefore I beg you to take some food, for this is for your safety; for not a hair will perish from any of your heads.” When he had said this, and had taken bread, he gave thanks to God in the presence of all, and he broke it, and began to eat. Then they all cheered up, and they also took food.
slang_text: Right as daylight was breaking, Paul begged everybody to grab some food, saying, "This makes fourteen days you've been white-knuckling it with zip to eat. So come on, eat something already -- this is for your own good, 'cause not one hair on your heads is gonna get messed with." Then he grabbed some bread, gave God his thanks right there in front of everybody, broke it, and dug in. That totally perked the whole crew up, and they all started eating too.

web_text: In all, we were two hundred seventy-six souls on the ship. When they had eaten enough, they lightened the ship, throwing out the wheat into the sea. When it was day, they didn’t recognize the land, but they noticed a certain bay with a beach, and they decided to try to drive the ship onto it. Casting off the anchors, they left them in the sea, at the same time untying the rudder ropes. Hoisting up the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach.
slang_text: Two hundred seventy-six of us total crammed on that ship. Once everybody'd had their fill, they dumped the wheat overboard to lighten the load. When it got light out, nobody had a clue what land this even was, but they spotted a bay with a beach and figured they'd try to run the ship straight up onto it. They cut the anchors loose and left 'em in the sea, untied the rudder ropes, hoisted the foresail up to catch the wind, and aimed her right for the beach.

web_text: But coming to a place where two seas met, they ran the vessel aground. The bow struck and remained immovable, but the stern began to break up by the violence of the waves. The soldiers’ counsel was to kill the prisoners, so that none of them would swim out and escape. But the centurion, desiring to save Paul, stopped them from their purpose, and commanded that those who could swim should throw themselves overboard first to go toward the land; and the rest should follow, some on planks, and some on other things from the ship. So they all escaped safely to the land.
slang_text: But right where two currents crashed together, the ship ran itself aground for real. The bow slammed in and stuck fast, goin' nowhere, while the stern started getting torn apart piece by piece by the pounding waves -- straight-up gnarly. The soldiers' bright idea was to just off the prisoners so none of 'em could swim away and bounce for freedom. But the centurion wanted Paul saved, so he shut that plan down cold, and instead ordered everybody who could swim to jump overboard first and make for shore, with the rest following on planks or whatever busted-up pieces of the ship they could grab. And that's how every single soul made it to land safe and sound.

web_text: When we had escaped, then they learned that the island was called Malta. The natives showed us uncommon kindness; for they kindled a fire, and received us all, because of the present rain, and because of the cold. But when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks and laid them on the fire, a viper came out because of the heat, and fastened on his hand. When the natives saw the creature hanging from his hand, they said to one another, “No doubt this man is a murderer, whom, though he has escaped from the sea, yet Justice has not allowed to live.”
slang_text: So once we made it to shore alive, we found out this island was called Malta. Those local dudes were totally righteous to us -- built us a fire and welcomed the whole gnarly crew, 'cause it was pouring rain and freezing cold. Paul's out there stacking sticks on the fire like a boss, when outta nowhere a viper bails outta the heat and latches onto his hand. The natives get a load of this snake hanging off him and start whispering, "No duh this guy's a murderer -- dude survived the shipwreck but Justice ain't lettin' him live anyway."

web_text: However he shook off the creature into the fire, and wasn’t harmed. But they expected that he would have swollen or fallen down dead suddenly, but when they watched for a long time and saw nothing bad happen to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a god. Now in the neighborhood of that place were lands belonging to the chief man of the island, named Publius, who received us, and courteously entertained us for three days. The father of Publius lay sick of fever and dysentery. Paul entered in to him, prayed, and laying his hands on him, healed him.
slang_text: But Paul just shakes the critter off into the fire, cool as you please, and doesn't even flinch. Everybody's standing there waiting for him to blow up like a balloon or drop dead on the spot, but after watching forever and seeing zip happen, they totally flip their story and decide he's some kind of god. No way, right? Now nearby was land belonging to Publius, the top dog of the island, and he took us in and hooked us up for three whole days, totally hospitable. Turns out Publius's dad was laid out sick with fever and dysentery. Paul goes in, prays over him, lays hands on the guy, and heals him -- just like that.

web_text: Then when this was done, the rest also who had diseases in the island came, and were cured. They also honored us with many honors, and when we sailed, they put on board the things that we needed. After three months, we set sail in a ship of Alexandria which had wintered in the island, whose sign was “The Twin Brothers.” Touching at Syracuse, we stayed there three days.
slang_text: Word got around after that, and the rest of the sick folks on the island came out and got cured too, totally epic. Those people honored us big time, and when we were ready to bounce, they loaded up the ship with everything we needed for the trip. After three months chillin' there, we set sail on an Alexandrian ship called "The Twin Brothers" that had wintered on the island. We touched down at Syracuse and hung out there three days.

web_text: From there we circled around and arrived at Rhegium. After one day, a south wind sprang up, and on the second day we came to Puteoli, where we found brothers, and were entreated to stay with them for seven days. So we came to Rome. From there the brothers, when they heard of us, came to meet us as far as The Market of Appius and The Three Taverns. When Paul saw them, he thanked God, and took courage. When we entered into Rome, the centurion delivered the prisoners to the captain of the guard, but Paul was allowed to stay by himself with the soldier who guarded him.
slang_text: From there we swung around and landed at Rhegium. One day later a south wind kicked up, and by the next day we hit Puteoli, where we found some brothers who begged us to crash with them a whole week -- totally choice. Then we made it to Rome. The brothers there heard we were coming and hiked all the way out to meet us at The Market of Appius and The Three Taverns. When Paul spotted them, he thanked God and got a serious boost of courage. Once we rolled into Rome, the centurion handed the prisoners over to the captain of the guard, but Paul got to crash on his own with just the soldier guarding him -- not bad, considering.

web_text: After three days Paul called together those who were the leaders of the Jews. When they had come together, he said to them, “I, brothers, though I had done nothing against the people, or the customs of our fathers, still was delivered prisoner from Jerusalem into the hands of the Romans, who, when they had examined me, desired to set me free, because there was no cause of death in me. But when the Jews spoke against it, I was constrained to appeal to Caesar, not that I had anything about which to accuse my nation. For this cause therefore I asked to see you and to speak with you. For because of the hope of Israel I am bound with this chain.”
slang_text: Three days later, Paul calls together the head honchos of the Jews there. Once they're all gathered, he lays it out: "Look, brothers, I never did anything against our people or the old ways of our ancestors, but still I got shipped off from Jerusalem as a prisoner, straight into Roman hands. They grilled me and wanted to cut me loose, 'cause there was nothing on me worth dying for. But when the Jews started trippin' and talking smack against it, I had no choice but to appeal to Caesar -- not that I got beef with my own nation, no way. That's exactly why I asked to see you all and talk face to face. Truth is, I'm wearing this chain because of the hope of Israel, plain and simple."

web_text: They said to him, “We neither received letters from Judea concerning you, nor did any of the brothers come here and report or speak any evil of you. But we desire to hear from you what you think. For, as concerning this sect, it is known to us that everywhere it is spoken against.” When they had appointed him a day, many people came to him at his lodging. He explained to them, testifying about God’s Kingdom, and persuading them concerning Jesus, both from the law of Moses and from the prophets, from morning until evening. Some believed the things which were spoken, and some disbelieved.
slang_text: They tell him, "We haven't gotten any letters from Judea about you, and none of the brothers who blew through here said one bad word about you either. But we're totally down to hear what you actually think, 'cause as far as this sect goes, everybody everywhere's talking trash about it, that much we know." So they set him up a day, and a bunch of people showed up at his place. He broke it down for them from morning to night, testifying about God's Kingdom and trying to convince 'em about Jesus, pulling straight from the Law of Moses and the prophets. Some of 'em believed what he said; others just weren't buying it, no duh.

web_text:
When they didn’t agree among themselves, they departed after Paul had spoken one word, “The Holy Spirit spoke rightly through Isaiah, the prophet, to our fathers, saying,
‘Go to this people, and say,
in hearing, you will hear,
but will in no way understand.
In seeing, you will see,
but will in no way perceive. For this people’s heart has grown callous.
Their ears are dull of hearing.
Their eyes they have closed.
Lest they should see with their eyes,
hear with their ears,
understand with their heart,
and would turn again,
and I would heal them.’ “Be it known therefore to you, that the salvation of God is sent to the nations, and they will listen.”
slang_text:
Since they couldn't get on the same page, they bounced after Paul dropped one last bomb on 'em: "The Holy Spirit totally called it right when he spoke through Isaiah the prophet to our ancestors, sayin':

'Go to this people and tell 'em,
you'll hear it loud and clear,
but you flat-out won't get it.
You'll see it plain as day,
but it won't sink in at all. 'Cause this people's hearts have gone grody and hard.
Their ears are stuffed up.
Their eyes are shut tight.
So they won't see with those eyes,
or hear with those ears,
or get it in their hearts,
and turn back around,
so I could heal 'em.'
So get a clue -- God's salvation just got sent out to the nations, and they're actually gonna listen up."

web_text: When he had said these words, the Jews departed, having a great dispute among themselves. Paul stayed two whole years in his own rented house, and received all who were coming to him, preaching God’s Kingdom, and teaching the things concerning the Lord Jesus Christ with all boldness, without hindrance.
slang_text: After that speech, the Jews took off, arguing hard among themselves the whole way. And Paul? He stuck it out two whole years in his own rented crib, welcoming anybody who came by, preaching God's Kingdom and teaching all about the Lord Jesus Christ with total boldness -- no one stopping him, no hindrance, no way. Totally unstoppable to the very end. Cowabunga.
